summarization.a: Figure 6 illustrates the effect of incomplete H-transfer on the ALD growth of alumina. Due to kinetic limitations at low temperatures, only half of the available H reacts during the H₂O pulse, resulting in partial CH₄ desorption. As a result, some CH₃ and OH remain on the surface ([CH₃] > 6.6 µmol/m², [OH] > 13 µmol/m²), which can lead to contamination and a reduced growth rate of approximately 0.8 Å/cycle, compared to the optimum conditions shown in Fig. 4.

data_extraction.a:
| Species | Coverage (µmol/m²) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| [CH₃] | >6.6 | Some unreacted CH₃ remains due to incomplete H-transfer |
| [OH] | >13 | Hydroxyl groups not fully consumed, may cause film contamination |
| H reacted | 50% | Only half of available H reacts to produce CH₄, compared to optimum |

a.question: Does incomplete H-transfer during ALD reduce the growth rate of alumina?

b.question: How does the growth rate at low H mobility compare to the optimum growth rate?
b.answer: The growth rate drops to approximately 0.8 Å/cycle, which is about half of the optimum rate.

d.question: Explain the consequences of kinetically-limited ALD on film quality and deposition
d.answer: Kinetically-limited ALD at low temperatures leads to incomplete H-transfer, leaving unreacted CH₃ and OH on the surface. This residual material can contaminate the growing alumina film and reduce the effective growth rate to about 0.8 Å/cycle. Such limitations are important for controlling film uniformity, purity, and overall deposition efficiency, especially when precise monolayer growth is required.

c.question: List the surface species affected by incomplete H-transfer during ALD.
c.answer: [CH₃] (residual methyl groups), [OH] (residual hydroxyl groups), H (only 50% reacts to form CH₄)
